sourceQuestion:
A B+ tree index is to be built on the Name attribute of the relation STUDENT. Assume that all the student names are of length 8 bytes, disk blocks are of size 512 bytes, and index pointers are of size 4 bytes. Given the scenario, what would be the best choice of the degree (i.e. number of pointers per node) of the B+ tree?

A. 16
B. 42
C. 43
D. 44

parsed.stem: A B+ tree index is to be built on the Name attribute of the relation STUDENT. Assume that all the student names are of length 8 bytes, disk blocks are of size 512 bytes, and index pointers are of size 4 bytes. Given the scenario, what would be the best choice of the degree (i.e. number of pointers per node) of the B+ tree?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following 2-3-4 tree (i.e., B-tree with a minimum degree of two) in which each data item is a letter. The usual alphabetical ordering of letters is used in constructing the tree.

The initial tree has root node [B, I, N] with children:
- Left child: [A]
- Second child: [E, G]
- Third child: [K, L, M]
- Right child: [P, T, X, Y]

What is the result of inserting G in the above tree?

A. (tree with root [B, I, N] and same structure but G inserted in [E, G] subtree producing a split)
B. (tree with root [G, I, N] and restructured subtree)
C. (tree with root [B, G, I, N])
D. None of the above

parsed.stem:
Consider the following 2-3-4 tree (i.e., B-tree with a minimum degree of two) in which each data item is a letter. The usual alphabetical ordering of letters is used in constructing the tree.

The initial tree has root node [B, I, N] with children:
- Left child: [A]
- Second child: [E, G]
- Third child: [K, L, M]
- Right child: [P, T, X, Y]

What is the result of inserting G in the above tree?

options.text: (tree with root [B, I, N] and same structure but G inserted in [E, G] subtree producing a split)
(tree with root [G, I, N] and restructured subtree)
(tree with root [B, G, I, N])
None of the above

sourceQuestion:
The order of an internal node in a B+ tree index is the maximum number of children it can have. Suppose that a child pointer takes 6 bytes, the search field value takes 14 bytes, and the block size is 512 bytes. What is the order of the internal node?

A. 24
B. 25
C. 26
D. 27

parsed.stem: The order of an internal node in a B+ tree index is the maximum number of children it can have. Suppose that a child pointer takes 6 bytes, the search field value takes 14 bytes, and the block size is 512 bytes. What is the order of the internal node?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is a key factor for preferring B+-trees to binary search trees for indexing database relations?

A. Database relations have a large number of records
B. Database relations are sorted on the primary key
C. B+-trees require less memory than binary search trees
D. Data transfer from disk to main memory is done in blocks
sourceAnswer: D. Data transfer from disk to main memory is done in blocks

parsed.stem: Which of the following is a key factor for preferring B+-trees to binary search trees for indexing database relations?

options.text: Database relations have a large number of records
Database relations are sorted on the primary key
B+-trees require less memory than binary search trees
Data transfer from disk to main memory is done in blocks

sourceQuestion:
The order of a leaf node in a B+ tree is the maximum number of (value, data record pointer) pairs it can hold. Given that the block size is 1K bytes, data record pointer is 7 bytes long, the value field is 9 bytes long and a block pointer is 6 bytes long, what is the order of the leaf node?
A. 63
B. 64
C. 67
D. 68

parsed.stem: The order of a leaf node in a B+ tree is the maximum number of (value, data record pointer) pairs it can hold. Given that the block size is 1K bytes, data record pointer is 7 bytes long, the value field is 9 bytes long and a block pointer is 6 bytes long, what is the order of the leaf node?

sourceQuestion:
A B-tree of order 4 is built from scratch by 10 successive insertions. What is the maximum number of node splitting operations that may take place?
A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 5

parsed.stem: A B-tree of order 4 is built from scratch by 10 successive insertions. What is the maximum number of node splitting operations that may take place?

sourceQuestion:
The following key values are inserted into a B+ tree in which order of the internal nodes is 3, and that of the leaf nodes is 5, in the sequence given below. The order of internal nodes is the maximum number of tree pointers in each node, and the order of leaf nodes is the maximum number of data items in each node. The B+ tree is initially empty.
10, 3, 6, 8, 4, 2, 1
The maximum number of tree nodes that would get split up as a result of these insertions is
A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 5

parsed.stem:
The following key values are inserted into a B+ tree in which order of the internal nodes is 3, and that of the leaf nodes is 5, in the sequence given below. The order of internal nodes is the maximum number of tree pointers in each node, and the order of leaf nodes is the maximum number of data items in each node. The B+ tree is initially empty.
10, 3, 6, 8, 4, 2, 1
The maximum number of tree nodes that would get split up as a result of these insertions is

sourceQuestion:
Consider a B+ tree in which the maximum number of keys in a node is 5. What is the minimum number of keys in any non-root node?
A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4

parsed.stem: Consider a B+ tree in which the maximum number of keys in a node is 5. What is the minimum number of keys in any non-root node?

sourceQuestion:
B+ Trees are considered BALANCED because
A. The lengths of the paths from the root to all leaf nodes are all equal.
B. The lengths of the paths from the root to all leaf nodes differ from each other by at most 1.
C. The number of children of any two non-leaf sibling nodes differ by at most 1.
D. The number of records in any two leaf nodes differ by at most 1.
sourceAnswer: A. The lengths of the paths from the root to all leaf nodes are all equal.

parsed.stem: B+ Trees are considered BALANCED because

options.text: The lengths of the paths from the root to all leaf nodes are all equal.
The lengths of the paths from the root to all leaf nodes differ from each other by at most 1.
The number of children of any two non-leaf sibling nodes differ by at most 1.
The number of records in any two leaf nodes differ by at most 1.

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following statements is NOT correct about the B+ tree data structure used for creating an index of a relational database table?
A. B+ tree is a height-balanced tree
B. Non-leaf nodes have pointers to data records
C. Key values in each node are kept in sorted order
D. Each leaf node has a pointer to the next leaf node
sourceAnswer: B. Non-leaf nodes have pointers to data records

parsed.stem: Which one of the following statements is NOT correct about the B+ tree data structure used for creating an index of a relational database table?

options.text: B+ tree is a height-balanced tree
Non-leaf nodes have pointers to data records
Key values in each node are kept in sorted order
Each leaf node has a pointer to the next leaf node

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following B+ tree with 5 nodes, in which a node can store at most 3 key values. The tree structure is:
  Root (internal): [19, 33, 44] (or similar based on image)
  Leaves: A = {9e, 77e, 81e}, B = {7e, 19e, 33e}, C = {20e, 22e, 29e}, and additional leaf nodes.
  (e-marked keys are data entries in a leaf)

Assume a pointer between keys k1 and k2 goes to a subtree containing keys between k1 and k2. When a leaf is found, the smallest key in the parent of the leaf that contains 25* is some value.

The value 23 is now inserted in the B+ tree. Which of the following option(s) is/are CORRECT?
A. None of the nodes will split.
B. Exactly one node will split.
C. The number of nodes will remain the same.
D. The height of the tree will increase.
sourceAnswer: B. Exactly one node will split.  D. The height of the tree will increase.

parsed.stem:
Consider the following B+ tree with 5 nodes, in which a node can store at most 3 key values. The tree structure is:
  Root (internal): [19, 33, 44] (or similar based on image)
  Leaves: A = {9e, 77e, 81e}, B = {7e, 19e, 33e}, C = {20e, 22e, 29e}, and additional leaf nodes.
  (e-marked keys are data entries in a leaf)

Assume a pointer between keys k1 and k2 goes to a subtree containing keys between k1 and k2. When a leaf is found, the smallest key in the parent of the leaf that contains 25* is some value.

The value 23 is now inserted in the B+ tree. Which of the following option(s) is/are CORRECT?

options.text: None of the nodes will split.
Exactly one node will split.
The number of nodes will remain the same.
The height of the tree will increase.

sourceQuestion:
Consider a table T in a relational database with a key field K. A B tree of order p is used as an access structure on K, where p denotes the maximum number of tree pointers in a B tree index node. Assume that K is 10 bytes long, disk block size is 512 bytes, each data pointer P_D is 8 bytes long and each block pointer P_B is 5 bytes long. In order for each B tree node to fit in one disk block, the maximum value of p is:

A. 21
B. 22
C. 23
D. 32

parsed.stem: Consider a table T in a relational database with a key field K. A B tree of order p is used as an access structure on K, where p denotes the maximum number of tree pointers in a B tree index node. Assume that K is 10 bytes long, disk block size is 512 bytes, each data pointer P_D is 8 bytes long and each block pointer P_B is 5 bytes long. In order for each B tree node to fit in one disk block, the maximum value of p is:

sourceQuestion:
A B tree used as an index for a large database table has four levels including the root node. If a new key is inserted in this index, the maximum number of nodes that could be newly created in the process are:

A. 5
B. 4
C. 3
D. 2

parsed.stem: A B tree used as an index for a large database table has four levels including the root node. If a new key is inserted in this index, the maximum number of nodes that could be newly created in the process are:

sourceQuestion:
In a database, the search key field is 9 bytes long, the block size is 512 bytes, a record pointer is 7 bytes and a block pointer is 6 bytes. The largest possible order of a non-leaf node in a B+ tree implementing this file structure is:

A. 25
B. 24
C. 34
D. 44

parsed.stem: In a database, the search key field is 9 bytes long, the block size is 512 bytes, a record pointer is 7 bytes and a block pointer is 6 bytes. The largest possible order of a non-leaf node in a B+ tree implementing this file structure is:

sourceQuestion:
Consider the B+ tree in the adjoining figure, where each node has at most two keys and three links.

The B+ tree shown has the following structure (order-3 B+ tree with max 2 keys per node):
- Root: [40]
- Left subtree of root: [10|20] with children [5|8], [12|15], [25|30]
- Right subtree of root: [50|60] with children [42|45], [55|57], [65|70]

Keys K=15 and then K=25 are inserted into this tree in that order. Exactly how many of the following nodes (disregarding the links) will be present in the tree after the two insertions?

[10|20], [12], [25|30], [50|60]

A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4

parsed.stem:
Consider the B+ tree in the adjoining figure, where each node has at most two keys and three links.

The B+ tree shown has the following structure (order-3 B+ tree with max 2 keys per node):
- Root: [40]
- Left subtree of root: [10|20] with children [5|8], [12|15], [25|30]
- Right subtree of root: [50|60] with children [42|45], [55|57], [65|70]

Keys K=15 and then K=25 are inserted into this tree in that order. Exactly how many of the following nodes (disregarding the links) will be present in the tree after the two insertions?

[10|20], [12], [25|30], [50|60]

sourceQuestion:
Consider the B+ tree in the adjoining figure, where each node has at most two keys and three links.

Keys K=15 and then K=25 are inserted into this tree in that order. Now the key K=50 is deleted from the B+ tree resulting after the two insertions made earlier. Consider the following statements about the B+ tree resulting after this deletion:

(i) The height of the tree remains the same.
(ii) The node [40|50] (disregarding the links) is present in the tree.
(iii) The root node remains unchanged (disregarding the links).

Which one of the following options is true?

A. Statements (i) and (ii) are true
B. Statements (i) and (iii) are true
C. Statements (ii) and (iii) are true
D. All the statements are false

parsed.stem:
Consider the B+ tree in the adjoining figure, where each node has at most two keys and three links.

Keys K=15 and then K=25 are inserted into this tree in that order. Now the key K=50 is deleted from the B+ tree resulting after the two insertions made earlier. Consider the following statements about the B+ tree resulting after this deletion:

(i) The height of the tree remains the same.
(ii) The node [40|50] (disregarding the links) is present in the tree.
(iii) The root node remains unchanged (disregarding the links).

Which one of the following options is true?

options.text: Statements (i) and (ii) are true
Statements (i) and (iii) are true
Statements (ii) and (iii) are true
All the statements are false

sourceQuestion:
Consider a relational table with a single record for each registered student with the following attributes:
1. Registration_Num: Unique registration number for each registered student
2. UID: Unique identity number, unique at the national level for each citizen
3. BankAccount_Num: Unique account number at the bank. A student can have multiple accounts or joint accounts. This attribute stores the primary account number.
4. Name: Name of the student
5. HostelRoom: Room number of the hostel

Which of the following options is INCORRECT?
A. BankAccount_Num is a candidate key
B. Name can be a primary key
C. UID is a candidate key if all students are from the same country
D. If S is a super key such that S ∩ UID = NULL then S ∪ UID is also a superkey
sourceAnswer: B. Name can be a primary key

parsed.stem:
Consider a relational table with a single record for each registered student with the following attributes:
1. Registration_Num: Unique registration number for each registered student
2. UID: Unique identity number, unique at the national level for each citizen
3. BankAccount_Num: Unique account number at the bank. A student can have multiple accounts or joint accounts. This attribute stores the primary account number.
4. Name: Name of the student
5. HostelRoom: Room number of the hostel

Which of the following options is INCORRECT?

options.text: BankAccount_Num is a candidate key
Name can be a primary key
UID is a candidate key if all students are from the same country
If S is a super key such that S ∩ UID = NULL then S ∪ UID is also a superkey

sourceQuestion:
A prime attribute of a relation scheme R is an attribute that appears
A. in all candidate keys of R
B. in some candidate key of R
C. in a foreign key of R
D. only in the primary key of R
sourceAnswer: B. in some candidate key of R

parsed.stem: A prime attribute of a relation scheme R is an attribute that appears

options.text: in all candidate keys of R
in some candidate key of R
in a foreign key of R
only in the primary key of R

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following four schedules due to three transactions (indicated by the subscript) using read and write on a data item x, denoted by r(x) and w(x) respectively. Which one of them is conflict serializable?
A. r_1(x); r_2(x); w_1(x); r_3(x); w_2(x)
B. r_2(x); r_3(x); w_2(x); r_1(x); w_3(x)
C. r_3(x); r_2(x); w_3(x); r_1(x); w_2(x)
D. r_2(x); r_1(x); w_2(x); r_3(x); w_1(x)

parsed.stem: Consider the following four schedules due to three transactions (indicated by the subscript) using read and write on a data item x, denoted by r(x) and w(x) respectively. Which one of them is conflict serializable?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following schedule S of transactions T1, T2, T3, T4:

Time | T1         | T2         | T3         | T4
-----|------------|------------|------------|------------
 1   |            | Reads(X)   |            |
 2   |            | Writes(X)  |            |
 3   |            | Commit     |            |
 4   |            |            | Writes(X)  |
 5   | Writes(X)  |            |            |
 6   |            |            | Writes(Y)  |
 7   |            |            | Reads(Z)   |
 8   |            |            | Commit     |
 9   |            |            |            | Reads(X)
10   |            |            |            | Reads(Y)
11   |            |            |            | Commit
12   | Commit     |            |            |

Which one of the following statements is CORRECT?
A. S is conflict-serializable but not recoverable
B. S is not conflict-serializable but is recoverable
C. S is both conflict-serializable and recoverable
D. S is neither conflict-serializable nor is it recoverable
sourceAnswer: D. S is neither conflict-serializable nor is it recoverable

parsed.stem:
Consider the following schedule S of transactions T1, T2, T3, T4:

Time | T1         | T2         | T3         | T4
-----|------------|------------|------------|------------
 1   |            | Reads(X)   |            |
 2   |            | Writes(X)  |            |
 3   |            | Commit     |            |
 4   |            |            | Writes(X)  |
 5   | Writes(X)  |            |            |
 6   |            |            | Writes(Y)  |
 7   |            |            | Reads(Z)   |
 8   |            |            | Commit     |
 9   |            |            |            | Reads(X)
10   |            |            |            | Reads(Y)
11   |            |            |            | Commit
12   | Commit     |            |            |

Which one of the following statements is CORRECT?

options.text: S is conflict-serializable but not recoverable
S is not conflict-serializable but is recoverable
S is both conflict-serializable and recoverable
S is neither conflict-serializable nor is it recoverable

sourceQuestion:
Consider the transactions T1, T2, and the schedules S1 and S2 given below.

T1 : r1(X); r1(Y); w1(X); w1(Z)
T2 : r2(Z); r2(Y); w2(Y); w2(X)

S1 : r1(X); r2(Z); r1(Y); r2(Y); w1(X); w2(Y); r2(X); w1(Z); w2(X)
S2 : r1(X); r2(Z); r2(Y); r1(Y); w1(X); r2(X); w2(Y); w1(Z); w2(X)

Which one of the following statements about the schedules is TRUE?
A. Only S1 is conflict-serializable.
B. Only S2 is conflict-serializable.
C. Both S1 and S2 are conflict-serializable.
D. Neither S1 nor S2 is conflict-serializable.
sourceAnswer: C. Both S1 and S2 are conflict-serializable.

parsed.stem:
Consider the transactions T1, T2, and the schedules S1 and S2 given below.

T1 : r1(X); r1(Y); w1(X); w1(Z)
T2 : r2(Z); r2(Y); w2(Y); w2(X)

S1 : r1(X); r2(Z); r1(Y); r2(Y); w1(X); w2(Y); r2(X); w1(Z); w2(X)
S2 : r1(X); r2(Z); r2(Y); r1(Y); w1(X); r2(X); w2(Y); w1(Z); w2(X)

Which one of the following statements about the schedules is TRUE?

options.text: Only S1 is conflict-serializable.
Only S2 is conflict-serializable.
Both S1 and S2 are conflict-serializable.
Neither S1 nor S2 is conflict-serializable.

sourceQuestion:
Let r_i(z) and w_i(z) denote read and write operations respectively on a data item z by a transaction T_i. Consider the following two schedules:
S1 : r1(a) r2(a) r3(b) r2(b) r1(b) w1(b) r3(a) w2(a)
S2 : r1(a) r2(a) r1(b) r3(b) r2(b) w1(b) r3(a) w2(a)
Which one of the following options is correct?
A. S1 is conflict serializable, and S2 is not conflict serializable
B. S1 is not conflict serializable, and S2 is conflict serializable
C. Both S1 and S2 are conflict serializable
D. Neither S1 nor S2 is conflict serializable
sourceAnswer: D. Neither S1 nor S2 is conflict serializable

parsed.stem:
Let r_i(z) and w_i(z) denote read and write operations respectively on a data item z by a transaction T_i. Consider the following two schedules:
S1 : r1(a) r2(a) r3(b) r2(b) r1(b) w1(b) r3(a) w2(a)
S2 : r1(a) r2(a) r1(b) r3(b) r2(b) w1(b) r3(a) w2(a)
Which one of the following options is correct?

options.text: S1 is conflict serializable, and S2 is not conflict serializable
S1 is not conflict serializable, and S2 is conflict serializable
Both S1 and S2 are conflict serializable
Neither S1 nor S2 is conflict serializable

sourceQuestion:
Let S be the following schedule of operations of three transactions T1, T2, and T3 in a relational database system:
S : R1(Y), R2(Y), R3(Y), W1(Y), W2(Y), R3(Z), W3(Z)
Consider the statements P and Q below:
P : S is conflict-serializable.
Q : If T2 commits before T3 finishes, then S is recoverable.
Which one of the following choices is correct?
A. Both P and Q are true
B. P is true and Q is false
C. P is false and Q is true
D. Both P and Q are false
sourceAnswer: A. Both P and Q are true

parsed.stem:
Let S be the following schedule of operations of three transactions T1, T2, and T3 in a relational database system:
S : R1(Y), R2(Y), R3(Y), W1(Y), W2(Y), R3(Z), W3(Z)
Consider the statements P and Q below:
P : S is conflict-serializable.
Q : If T2 commits before T3 finishes, then S is recoverable.
Which one of the following choices is correct?

options.text: Both P and Q are true
P is true and Q is false
P is false and Q is true
Both P and Q are false

sourceQuestion:
Let R_i(z) and W_i(z) denote read and write operations on a data element z by a transaction T_i respectively. Consider the schedule S with four transactions:
S : R1(X) R2(X) W1(X) W2(Y) W1(Y) R3(X) W3(Y) R4(X) W4(Y) COMMIT(T1) COMMIT(T2)
Which one of the following serial schedules is conflict equivalent to S?
A. T1 -> T2 -> T3 -> T4
B. T2 -> T1 -> T3 -> T4
C. T1 -> T3 -> T2 -> T4
D. T1 -> T2 -> T4 -> T3

parsed.stem:
Let R_i(z) and W_i(z) denote read and write operations on a data element z by a transaction T_i respectively. Consider the schedule S with four transactions:
S : R1(X) R2(X) W1(X) W2(Y) W1(Y) R3(X) W3(Y) R4(X) W4(Y) COMMIT(T1) COMMIT(T2)
Which one of the following serial schedules is conflict equivalent to S?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following read-write schedule S over three transactions T1, T2, and T3, where the subscripts in the schedule indicate transaction IDs:
S: r1(x), r3(x), w3(y), w1(y), r2(y), r2(y), w2(y), w2(y)
Which of the following transaction schedules is/are conflict equivalent to S?
A. T1, T2, T3
B. T2, T1, T3
C. T1, T3, T2
D. T1, T2, T3

parsed.stem:
Consider the following read-write schedule S over three transactions T1, T2, and T3, where the subscripts in the schedule indicate transaction IDs:
S: r1(x), r3(x), w3(y), w1(y), r2(y), r2(y), w2(y), w2(y)
Which of the following transaction schedules is/are conflict equivalent to S?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the database transactions T1 and T2, and data items X and Y.

Transaction T1:       Transaction T2:
R1(X)                 W2(X)
W1(Y)                 W2(Y)
R1(X)                 COMMIT(T2)
W1(X)
COMMIT(T1)

Which of the schedule(s) is/are conflict serializable?
A. R1(X), W2(X), W1(Y), W2(Y), R1(X), W1(X), COMMIT(T2), COMMIT(T1)
B. W2(X), R1(X), W2(Y), W1(Y), R1(X), COMMIT(T2), W1(X), COMMIT(T1)
C. R1(X), W1(Y), W2(X), W2(Y), R1(X), W1(X), COMMIT(T2), COMMIT(T1)
D. W2(X), R1(X), W2(Y), W1(Y), W1(X), COMMIT(T2), R1(X), COMMIT(T1)

parsed.stem:
Consider the database transactions T1 and T2, and data items X and Y.

Transaction T1:       Transaction T2:
R1(X)                 W2(X)
W1(Y)                 W2(Y)
R1(X)                 COMMIT(T2)
W1(X)
COMMIT(T1)

Which of the schedule(s) is/are conflict serializable?

options.text: R1(X), W2(X), W1(Y), W2(Y), R1(X), W1(X), COMMIT(T2), COMMIT(T1)
W2(X), R1(X), W2(Y), W1(Y), R1(X), COMMIT(T2), W1(X), COMMIT(T1)
R1(X), W1(Y), W2(X), W2(Y), R1(X), W1(X), COMMIT(T2), COMMIT(T1)
W2(X), R1(X), W2(Y), W1(Y), W1(X), COMMIT(T2), R1(X), COMMIT(T1)

sourceQuestion:
Match the pairs in the following questions:

Group 1:
(a) Secondary Index
(b) Non-procedural query language
(c) Closure of a set of attributes
(d) Natural join

Group 2:
(p) Function dependency
(q) B-tree
(r) Domain calculus
(s) Relational algebraic operations

A. a-q, b-r, c-p, d-s
B. a-p, b-r, c-q, d-s
C. a-q, b-p, c-r, d-s
D. a-r, b-q, c-p, d-s

parsed.stem:
Match the pairs in the following questions:

Group 1:
(a) Secondary Index
(b) Non-procedural query language
(c) Closure of a set of attributes
(d) Natural join

Group 2:
(p) Function dependency
(q) B-tree
(r) Domain calculus
(s) Relational algebraic operations

options.text: a-q, b-r, c-p, d-s
a-p, b-r, c-q, d-s
a-q, b-p, c-r, d-s
a-r, b-q, c-p, d-s

sourceQuestion:
Indicate which of the following statements are true:
A relational database which is in 3NF may still have undesirable data redundancy because there may exist:
A. Transitive functional dependencies
B. Non-trivial functional dependencies involving prime attributes on the right-side.
C. Non-trivial functional dependencies involving prime attributes only on the left-side.
D. Non-trivial functional dependencies involving only prime attributes.
sourceAnswer: B. Non-trivial functional dependencies involving prime attributes on the right-side.

parsed.stem:
Indicate which of the following statements are true:
A relational database which is in 3NF may still have undesirable data redundancy because there may exist:

options.text: Transitive functional dependencies
Non-trivial functional dependencies involving prime attributes on the right-side.
Non-trivial functional dependencies involving prime attributes only on the left-side.
Non-trivial functional dependencies involving only prime attributes.

sourceQuestion:
State True or False with reason:

There is always a decomposition into Boyce-Codd normal form (BCNF) that is lossless and dependency preserving.

A. True, because BCNF decomposition always preserves all FDs
B. False, because a lossless BCNF decomposition may not preserve all functional dependencies
C. True, because 3NF decomposition is always lossless and dependency-preserving
D. False, because BCNF decomposition is never lossless
sourceAnswer: B. False, because a lossless BCNF decomposition may not preserve all functional dependencies

parsed.stem:

True, because 3NF decomposition is always lossless and dependency-preserving
False, because BCNF decomposition is never lossless

sourceQuestion:
For a database relation R(a, b, c, d), where the domains of a, b, c, d include only atomic values, only the following functional dependencies and those that can be inferred from them hold:
- a -> c
- b -> d

This relation is

A. in first normal form but not in second normal form
B. in second normal form but not in first normal form
C. in third normal form
D. none of the above
sourceAnswer: A. in first normal form but not in second normal form
